name: Building Services (Construction)
context:
Devices: College desktop PCs and laptop banks. Interactive smartboards and projectors. MS Teams-enabled devices. Mobile digital testing equipment and diagnostic tools in workshops.

Network: Inconsistent Wi-Fi in some workshop and practical spaces. Connectivity drops when multiple learners access online systems simultaneously. Mobile signal limitations in some areas.

AT available: Microsoft Immersive Reader, speech-to-text, Read Aloud, font/colour accessibility settings, Learning Support interventions.

Accessibility barriers: Limited learner confidence and independence with digital systems. Home device access for some learners. Literacy difficulties impacting text-heavy digital resources. Inconsistent Wi-Fi. Practical workshop environments not fully designed for digital integration.

Devices available: Learners currently have access to a range of digital devices and technologies across construction and building services, including:

College desktop PCs and laptop banks within classrooms and workshops.
Interactive smartboards and projector systems within teaching spaces.
MS Teams-enabled devices for blended learning, online assignments, and communication.
Mobile digital testing equipment and diagnostic tools within electrical and plumbing workshops.
Digital portfolio access through learner devices and college systems.
Specialist workshop equipment with integrated digital controls and testing functions.
Access to online learning platforms and digital manufacturer resources through classroom and workshop devices.
QR code systems used for learner evidence capture, induction activities, and digital learning resources.

Some curriculum areas also have access to sector-relevant digital technologies linked to modern installation, testing, and fault-finding practices.

Devices needed: To further improve learner outcomes and align with modern industry expectations, the following equipment and upgrades would significantly strengthen delivery:

Increased availability of laptop banks and mobile devices for workshop-based learning and assessment.
Additional smartboards/screens in practical teaching areas to improve live demonstrations and access to digital content.
Updated industry-standard testing and diagnostic equipment aligned to current industry practice.
Renewable technology training rigs, including Solar PV and smart technology systems, to support emerging curriculum areas.
Improved access to CAD-capable devices and higher-specification computers for design and planning activities.
VR or simulation-based training equipment to safely support high-risk practical scenarios and immersive learning.
Better integration of digital technologies within workshops to mirror modern industry environments.
Replacement of ageing hardware in some teaching spaces where performance impacts teaching efficiency and learner engagement.

Network/connectivity: There are some ongoing connectivity and infrastructure challenges within teaching and workshop areas, including:

Inconsistent Wi-Fi coverage in some workshop and practical spaces.
Connectivity drops when multiple learners access online systems simultaneously.
Mobile signal limitations in some areas impacting authentication and online access.
Limited access to reliable printing in certain curriculum spaces.

Improved Wi-Fi infrastructure, stronger workshop connectivity, and better access to digital presentation systems would significantly improve digital teaching consistency.

Software available: earners currently have access to a broad range of digital platforms and software, including:

Microsoft 365 suite including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and OneDrive.
MS Teams for communication, assignment submission, online learning, and collaborative work.
Learning Assistant and Smart Assessor for portfolio evidence, assessment tracking, and learner feedback.
EBS/Tribal systems linked to attendance, progress tracking, and learner records.
Online awarding organisation resources and digital learning materials.
Manufacturer technical resources, online regulations, and installation guidance.
Digital testing and diagnostic interfaces linked to workshop equipment.
Online learning platforms supporting English, maths, safeguarding, and personal development.
CAD/drawing interpretation tools in relevant curriculum areas.
Digital enrichment and virtual work experience platforms where appropriate.

Software gaps: Greater access to industry-standard CAD and building services design software.
Simulation software linked to electrical fault finding, renewable technologies, and smart systems.
Expanded access to BIM-related technologies and digital construction platforms.
Renewable energy system software and monitoring platforms linked to Solar PV and low-carbon technologies.
Improved digital diagnostic and commissioning software aligned with modern building services industries.
AI-supported learning and assessment tools with clear educational guidance and safeguards.
More integrated digital systems linking workshop practical activity with live digital reporting and assessment.
Enhanced VR/simulation platforms to support safe rehearsal of technical and high-risk activities.

Assistive technology: Learners currently have access to a range of assistive technologies and accessibility support tools, including:

Microsoft Immersive Reader.
Speech-to-text and dictation tools within Microsoft 365.
Read Aloud functionality and accessibility features within Teams and Office applications.
Adjustments to font size, colour overlays, and screen accessibility settings.
Learning Support interventions and adapted teaching resources.
Use of laptops and digital devices to support learners with literacy or recording difficulties.
Captioning and transcription tools within Teams.
Flexible access to online learning materials allowing learners to revisit content independently.
Additional learning support staff working alongside learners where required.

Support is also provided through differentiated teaching approaches, scaffolded resources, and flexible assessment methods where appropriate.

Accessibility barriers: Current barriers include:

Limited learner confidence and independence with digital systems.
Access to suitable devices outside of college for some learners.
Literacy and reading difficulties impacting engagement with text-heavy digital resources.
Learners struggling to navigate multiple digital platforms and systems simultaneously.
Inconsistent Wi-Fi or connectivity affecting access during lessons.
Some practical workshop environments not being fully designed for integrated digital delivery.
Limited availability of specialist assistive technologies for higher-need learners.
Difficulties balancing practical learning with extended screen-based tasks for some SEND learners.
Staff confidence varying in adapting digital content for accessibility and inclusion.

To improve accessibility further, the faculty would benefit from:

Increased assistive technology availability and awareness.
More consistent accessible resource design across curriculum areas.
Improved workshop-based digital infrastructure.
Additional staff CPD around adaptive digital teaching strategies.
Simplified and more consistent digital learner journeys across platforms and systems.

Teams/DLE: MS Teams is currently used across the faculty as a central platform for communication, assignment distribution, learning resources, online submissions, learner support, and blended delivery. Staff use Teams to share lesson materials, PowerPoints, videos, workshop guidance, revision resources, and assessment information. It is also used to support remote access to learning, tutorial activity, and communication between staff and learners.

The platform is functioning well overall and has become embedded within day-to-day delivery. Learners are generally confident accessing Teams for assignments, messages, and learning materials.

Strengths include:

Consistent communication between staff and learners.
Centralised access to learning resources and assignments.
Support for blended and independent learning.
Improved tracking of learner engagement and submissions.
Integration with Microsoft 365 tools.

Current gaps and challenges include:

Inconsistent use across curriculum areas and individual staff.
Variable learner engagement with independent online learning.
Connectivity limitations in workshop spaces affecting live use.
Some practical areas lacking screens or devices to fully integrate digital teaching.
Need for further development of interactive and industry-linked digital content rather than static resource storage.
Greater integration needed between Teams, digital portfolios, and practical workshop delivery.

Overall, Teams provides a strong foundation, but continued development of digital pedagogy, infrastructure, and consistency would further improve learner outcomes.

Additional comments: One of the main challenges within construction and building services is balancing practical, hands-on delivery with meaningful digital integration. Learners still require significant workshop time to develop technical competence, therefore digital tools must enhance delivery rather than replace practical learning.

There is also a growing gap between rapidly evolving industry technologies and the pace at which education environments can update equipment, software, and staff training. Emerging areas such as renewable technologies, smart systems, AI, BIM, and digital diagnostics are becoming increasingly important within industry, and continued investment will be essential to ensure learners remain industry-ready.

Consistency is another challenge. While there is strong digital practice across many areas, approaches can vary between staff and curriculum teams. Continued CPD, clearer expectations, and improved infrastructure would help embed a more consistent learner experience across the faculty.

Finally, workshop environments often present practical barriers to digital delivery due to connectivity, device availability, and room design. Improving these areas would significantly strengthen blended learning and independent learner development.

Self-reported barrier impact (1=significant, 5=none):
Device access barrier: Moderate impact
Software barrier: Some impact
Staff capability barrier: Moderate impact
Network barrier: Low impact
strengths:
Clear evidence that digital development is taking place. Platforms such as MS Teams, Learning Assistant, Smart Assessor, online learning resources and digital testing equipment now embedded in day-to-day delivery.

Staff readiness: Strengths: Strong use of digital portfolio systems and online evidence tracking. Increasing use of digital teaching resources. Good engagement with Teams and blended learning.
Gaps: Consistency in embedding advanced digital skills. AI tools confidence. Industry-standard software.

What's working: There is clear evidence that digital development is taking place across the faculty, and learners are regularly exposed to a range of digital systems and technologies through their programmes. Platforms such as MS Teams, Learning Assistant, Smart Assessor, online learning resources, digital testing equipment, and manufacturer guidance are now embedded within day-to-day delivery and assessment practices.

However, there is an important distinction between learners using digital tools and learners genuinely developing digital capability. In many cases, digital systems are currently being used as operational tools to support delivery, administration, evidence collection, communication, or efficiency, rather than as part of a fully planned strategy to intentionally build learners' long-term digital skills and independence.

For example:

Learners upload evidence digitally, but this does not always mean they understand wider digital organisation, data management, or professional digital working practices.
Learners access Teams and online resources, but independent digital research and critical evaluation skills are still developing.
Staff increasingly use digital platforms to streamline teaching and assessment, though the learner digital skill development behind these activities can sometimes be implicit rather than explicit.
Digital technologies are often used to support completion and compliance, rather than fully preparing learners for rapidly changing digital expectations within industry.

That said, there are positive examples emerging:

Learners are becoming more confident using online technical resources, digital communication tools, and manufacturer guidance.
Digital portfolios are improving learner ownership of assessment evidence and progress tracking.
Some curriculum areas are naturally embedding industry-relevant technologies, particularly around diagnostics, testing equipment, and digital documentation.

The key next step is moving from "digital use" to "digital fluency," where digital skill development becomes intentional, visible, progressive, and directly linked to employability, problem solving, independent learning, and modern industry expectations.

Staff readiness: Strengths include:

Strong use of digital portfolio systems and online evidence tracking.
Increasing use of digital teaching resources, online videos, and manufacturer guidance.
Good engagement with digital assessment and feedback platforms.
Positive adoption of Teams and blended learning approaches.

Current gaps and development areas include:

Consistency in embedding advanced digital skills across all curriculum areas.
Confidence using AI tools effectively and safely within education and industry contexts.
Wider use of industry-standard software and emerging technologies linked to modern construction methods and smart technologies.
Staff confidence in developing higher-level learner digital independence rather than relying on tutor-led approaches.
Continued CPD needed around cyber awareness, digital safeguarding, and evolving industry technologies.

afiPhases.title: Coaching Support Plan
afiPhases.description: Support requested: Support to strategically plan, embed, and deliver digital fluency within both classroom and workshop environments — not just devices or software.

afiPhases.title: Address audit-identified priorities
afiPhases.description:
Support requested by area: The biggest area of support needed is not simply access to more devices, software, or hardware -- it is support to strategically plan, embed, and deliver digital fluency within both classroom and workshop environments.

Technology on its own does not automatically improve learning. Without clear curriculum planning, staff confidence, and ongoing support, digital tools risk becoming little more than administrative systems or isolated activities rather than meaningful drivers of learner development. To have real impact, digital skills need to be intentionally mapped into schemes of learning, workshop practice, assessment, employer expectations, and progression pathways.

Support is therefore needed in several connected areas:

Structured curriculum planning support to help staff identify where digital fluency naturally fits within practical and theoretical delivery.
CPD focused not just on "how to use the technology," but how to teach and develop digital capability progressively and meaningfully.
Guidance on embedding digital skills within workshop activities, fault finding, diagnostics, technical research, communication, AI literacy, and problem solving.
Time for curriculum teams to collaboratively design digital learning approaches and share best practice.
Leadership and strategic direction to ensure digital development is consistent, visible, and aligned to industry expectations.
Ongoing coaching and support for staff to move from digital compliance to digitally enhanced pedagogy.
Reliable infrastructure, devices, software, and workshop connectivity to allow digital approaches to happen seamlessly and consistently.

The ultimate goal should be creating digitally fluent learners who can confidently operate within modern industry environments, rather than learners who have simply used digital systems during their course. This requires a long-term cultural and curriculum shift, not just investment in technology alone.

activityLog.notes:
HoA Digital Skills & Infrastructure Audit response — submitted by Richard Hanney.

Core digital skills for learners: Digital Communication & Professional Conduct
Learners must be able to communicate professionally using email, MS Teams, online learning platforms, and digital job application systems. This includes submitting work digitally, responding to employer communications, and using collaborative platforms safely and professionally.
AI Literacy & Safe Use of Digital Tools
Learners should understand how to use AI tools responsibly to support learning, problem-solving, revision, planning, and research, whilst recognising issues around accuracy, plagiarism, safeguarding, and professional ethics within the construction and building services industries.
Digital Documentation & Data Handling
Learners need confidence using digital systems such as online portfolios, digital markbooks, QR-based evidence collection, spreadsheets, and cloud storage. They should be able to upload evidence, track progress, complete digital forms, and interpret basic performance or project data.
Industry-Specific Digital Systems & Software
Learners should develop competence in sector-specific technologies such as digital testing equipment, smart diagnostic tools, CAD/drawing interpretation software, online regulations/resources, Smart Assessor, Learning Assistant, and digital installation manuals used within construction and building services.
Digital Problem Solving & Technical Research
Learners must be able to independently research technical information online, interpret manufacturers' guidance, use digital fault-finding resources, and safely access current regulations, standards, and installation guidance relevant to their trade area.

How skills are baseline assessed: Digital skills are currently assessed through a range of methods across the learner journey:

Initial assessment and induction activities to identify learner confidence with digital platforms, email, Teams, and online systems.
Observation of learners using systems such as Learning Assistant, Smart Assessor, online submissions, and digital learning resources during lessons and workshop activity.
Portfolio evidence uploads and completion of digital assignments/projects.
Employer and work placement feedback linked to professionalism, communication, and use of workplace technology.
End-of-unit practical assessments where learners use digital tools, online guidance, testing equipment, or digital documentation.
Ongoing tutorial discussions and progress reviews identifying learner confidence and development needs in digital skills.
Tracking engagement with online learning platforms, independent study resources, and digital enrichment activities.

Staff capability needed: Staff need a strong blend of general and industry-specific digital capability to deliver these skills effectively, including:

Confidence using digital teaching platforms such as MS Teams, Learning Assistant, Smart Assessor, and online assessment systems.
Ability to model professional digital communication and digital working practices expected within industry.
Competence using sector-specific digital tools, testing equipment, diagnostic systems, online regulations, and digital manufacturer resources.
Understanding of AI tools and how to integrate them safely and ethically into teaching, learning, assessment, and learner support.
Ability to troubleshoot common learner digital issues and support learners with varying levels of digital confidence.
Skills in digital assessment, online feedback, evidence tracking, and use of data to monitor learner progress.
Confidence embedding digital skills naturally into workshop and classroom delivery rather than treating them as standalone activities.

Potential impact if barriers resolved: If the current barriers around devices, infrastructure, software access, and staff development were addressed, the impact on learner outcomes could be significant across both achievement and long-term employability.

Most importantly, digital development could move from being largely operational and compliance-driven to becoming intentional, progressive, and fully embedded within curriculum design and industry preparation. Learners would not simply be using digital tools because they have to -- they would be developing genuine digital fluency linked to modern workplace expectations.

This would lead to:

Greater learner independence, confidence, and problem-solving ability.
Stronger preparation for apprenticeships, employment, and progression into increasingly digital industries.
Improved learner engagement through more interactive, visual, and industry-relevant learning experiences.
Better integration of modern construction technologies such as renewable systems, smart diagnostics, BIM, digital reporting, and AI-supported workflows.
More consistent digital experiences across curriculum areas and teaching teams.
Improved accessibility and support for learners with SEND or lower digital starting points.
Increased opportunities for blended learning, revision, remote access, and flexible support.
Stronger employer confidence in learners' readiness for modern working environments.

Support requested: The biggest area of support needed is not simply access to more devices, software, or hardware -- it is support to strategically plan, embed, and deliver digital fluency within both classroom and workshop environments.

Technology on its own does not automatically improve learning. Without clear curriculum planning, staff confidence, and ongoing support, digital tools risk becoming little more than administrative systems or isolated activities rather than meaningful drivers of learner development. To have real impact, digital skills need to be intentionally mapped into schemes of learning, workshop practice, assessment, employer expectations, and progression pathways.

Support is therefore needed in several connected areas:

Structured curriculum planning support to help staff identify where digital fluency naturally fits within practical and theoretical delivery.
CPD focused not just on "how to use the technology," but how to teach and develop digital capability progressively and meaningfully.
Guidance on embedding digital skills within workshop activities, fault finding, diagnostics, technical research, communication, AI literacy, and problem solving.
Time for curriculum teams to collaboratively design digital learning approaches and share best practice.
Leadership and strategic direction to ensure digital development is consistent, visible, and aligned to industry expectations.
Ongoing coaching and support for staff to move from digital compliance to digitally enhanced pedagogy.
Reliable infrastructure, devices, software, and workshop connectivity to allow digital approaches to happen seamlessly and consistently.

The ultimate goal should be creating digitally fluent learners who can confidently operate within modern industry environments, rather than learners who have simply used digital systems during their course. This requires a long-term cultural and curriculum shift, not just investment in technology alone.

generalDigitalSkills.progressData:
There is clear evidence that digital development is taking place across the faculty, and learners are regularly exposed to a range of digital systems and technologies through their programmes. Platforms such as MS Teams, Learning Assistant, Smart Assessor, online learning resources, digital testing equipment, and manufacturer guidance are now embedded within day-to-day delivery and assessment practices.

However, there is an important distinction between learners using digital tools and learners genuinely developing digital capability. In many cases, digital systems are currently being used as operational tools to support delivery, administration, evidence collection, communication, or efficiency, rather than as part of a fully planned strategy to intentionally build learners' long-term digital skills and independence.

For example:

Learners upload evidence digitally, but this does not always mean they understand wider digital organisation, data management, or professional digital working practices.
Learners access Teams and online resources, but independent digital research and critical evaluation skills are still developing.
Staff increasingly use digital platforms to streamline teaching and assessment, though the learner digital skill development behind these activities can sometimes be implicit rather than explicit.
Digital technologies are often used to support completion and compliance, rather than fully preparing learners for rapidly changing digital expectations within industry.

That said, there are positive examples emerging:

Learners are becoming more confident using online technical resources, digital communication tools, and manufacturer guidance.
Digital portfolios are improving learner ownership of assessment evidence and progress tracking.
Some curriculum areas are naturally embedding industry-relevant technologies, particularly around diagnostics, testing equipment, and digital documentation.

The key next step is moving from "digital use" to "digital fluency," where digital skill development becomes intentional, visible, progressive, and directly linked to employability, problem solving, independent learning, and modern industry expectations.

industryDigitalSkills.curriculumSkillsList:
Digital Communication & Professional Conduct
Learners must be able to communicate professionally using email, MS Teams, online learning platforms, and digital job application systems. This includes submitting work digitally, responding to employer communications, and using collaborative platforms safely and professionally.
AI Literacy & Safe Use of Digital Tools
Learners should understand how to use AI tools responsibly to support learning, problem-solving, revision, planning, and research, whilst recognising issues around accuracy, plagiarism, safeguarding, and professional ethics within the construction and building services industries.
Digital Documentation & Data Handling
Learners need confidence using digital systems such as online portfolios, digital markbooks, QR-based evidence collection, spreadsheets, and cloud storage. They should be able to upload evidence, track progress, complete digital forms, and interpret basic performance or project data.
Industry-Specific Digital Systems & Software
Learners should develop competence in sector-specific technologies such as digital testing equipment, smart diagnostic tools, CAD/drawing interpretation software, online regulations/resources, Smart Assessor, Learning Assistant, and digital installation manuals used within construction and building services.
Digital Problem Solving & Technical Research
Learners must be able to independently research technical information online, interpret manufacturers' guidance, use digital fault-finding resources, and safely access current regulations, standards, and installation guidance relevant to their trade area.

Portfolio evidence uploads and completion of digital assignments/projects.
Employer and work placement feedback linked to professionalism, communication, and use of workplace technology.
End-of-unit practical assessments where learners use digital tools, online guidance, testing equipment, or digital documentation.
Ongoing tutorial discussions and progress reviews identifying learner confidence and development needs in digital skills.
Tracking engagement with online learning platforms, independent study resources, and digital enrichment activities.

industryDigitalSkills.progressData:
If the current barriers around devices, infrastructure, software access, and staff development were addressed, the impact on learner outcomes could be significant across both achievement and long-term employability.

Most importantly, digital development could move from being largely operational and compliance-driven to becoming intentional, progressive, and fully embedded within curriculum design and industry preparation. Learners would not simply be using digital tools because they have to -- they would be developing genuine digital fluency linked to modern workplace expectations.

This would lead to:

Greater learner independence, confidence, and problem-solving ability.
Stronger preparation for apprenticeships, employment, and progression into increasingly digital industries.
Improved learner engagement through more interactive, visual, and industry-relevant learning experiences.
Better integration of modern construction technologies such as renewable systems, smart diagnostics, BIM, digital reporting, and AI-supported workflows.
More consistent digital experiences across curriculum areas and teaching teams.
Improved accessibility and support for learners with SEND or lower digital starting points.
Increased opportunities for blended learning, revision, remote access, and flexible support.
Stronger employer confidence in learners' readiness for modern working environments.

name: Construction Trades
context:
Devices: College desktop PCs and laptop banks. Interactive smartboards and projectors. MS Teams-enabled devices. Mobile digital testing equipment and diagnostic tools in workshops.

Network: Inconsistent Wi-Fi in some workshop and practical spaces. Connectivity drops when multiple learners access online systems simultaneously. Mobile signal limitations in some areas.

AT available: Microsoft Immersive Reader, speech-to-text, Read Aloud, font/colour accessibility settings, Learning Support interventions.

Accessibility barriers: Limited learner confidence and independence with digital systems. Home device access for some learners. Literacy difficulties impacting text-heavy digital resources. Inconsistent Wi-Fi. Practical workshop environments not fully designed for digital integration.

Devices available: Learners currently have access to a range of digital devices and technologies across construction and building services, including:

College desktop PCs and laptop banks within classrooms and workshops.
Interactive smartboards and projector systems within teaching spaces.
MS Teams-enabled devices for blended learning, online assignments, and communication.
Mobile digital testing equipment and diagnostic tools within electrical and plumbing workshops.
Digital portfolio access through learner devices and college systems.
Specialist workshop equipment with integrated digital controls and testing functions.
Access to online learning platforms and digital manufacturer resources through classroom and workshop devices.
QR code systems used for learner evidence capture, induction activities, and digital learning resources.

Some curriculum areas also have access to sector-relevant digital technologies linked to modern installation, testing, and fault-finding practices.

Devices needed: To further improve learner outcomes and align with modern industry expectations, the following equipment and upgrades would significantly strengthen delivery:

Increased availability of laptop banks and mobile devices for workshop-based learning and assessment.
Additional smartboards/screens in practical teaching areas to improve live demonstrations and access to digital content.
Updated industry-standard testing and diagnostic equipment aligned to current industry practice.
Renewable technology training rigs, including Solar PV and smart technology systems, to support emerging curriculum areas.
Improved access to CAD-capable devices and higher-specification computers for design and planning activities.
VR or simulation-based training equipment to safely support high-risk practical scenarios and immersive learning.
Better integration of digital technologies within workshops to mirror modern industry environments.
Replacement of ageing hardware in some teaching spaces where performance impacts teaching efficiency and learner engagement.

Network/connectivity: There are some ongoing connectivity and infrastructure challenges within teaching and workshop areas, including:

Inconsistent Wi-Fi coverage in some workshop and practical spaces.
Connectivity drops when multiple learners access online systems simultaneously.
Mobile signal limitations in some areas impacting authentication and online access.
Limited access to reliable printing in certain curriculum spaces.

Improved Wi-Fi infrastructure, stronger workshop connectivity, and better access to digital presentation systems would significantly improve digital teaching consistency.

Software available: earners currently have access to a broad range of digital platforms and software, including:

Microsoft 365 suite including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and OneDrive.
MS Teams for communication, assignment submission, online learning, and collaborative work.
Learning Assistant and Smart Assessor for portfolio evidence, assessment tracking, and learner feedback.
EBS/Tribal systems linked to attendance, progress tracking, and learner records.
Online awarding organisation resources and digital learning materials.
Manufacturer technical resources, online regulations, and installation guidance.
Digital testing and diagnostic interfaces linked to workshop equipment.
Online learning platforms supporting English, maths, safeguarding, and personal development.
CAD/drawing interpretation tools in relevant curriculum areas.
Digital enrichment and virtual work experience platforms where appropriate.

Software gaps: Greater access to industry-standard CAD and building services design software.
Simulation software linked to electrical fault finding, renewable technologies, and smart systems.
Expanded access to BIM-related technologies and digital construction platforms.
Renewable energy system software and monitoring platforms linked to Solar PV and low-carbon technologies.
Improved digital diagnostic and commissioning software aligned with modern building services industries.
AI-supported learning and assessment tools with clear educational guidance and safeguards.
More integrated digital systems linking workshop practical activity with live digital reporting and assessment.
Enhanced VR/simulation platforms to support safe rehearsal of technical and high-risk activities.

Assistive technology: Learners currently have access to a range of assistive technologies and accessibility support tools, including:

Microsoft Immersive Reader.
Speech-to-text and dictation tools within Microsoft 365.
Read Aloud functionality and accessibility features within Teams and Office applications.
Adjustments to font size, colour overlays, and screen accessibility settings.
Learning Support interventions and adapted teaching resources.
Use of laptops and digital devices to support learners with literacy or recording difficulties.
Captioning and transcription tools within Teams.
Flexible access to online learning materials allowing learners to revisit content independently.
Additional learning support staff working alongside learners where required.

Support is also provided through differentiated teaching approaches, scaffolded resources, and flexible assessment methods where appropriate.

Accessibility barriers: Current barriers include:

Limited learner confidence and independence with digital systems.
Access to suitable devices outside of college for some learners.
Literacy and reading difficulties impacting engagement with text-heavy digital resources.
Learners struggling to navigate multiple digital platforms and systems simultaneously.
Inconsistent Wi-Fi or connectivity affecting access during lessons.
Some practical workshop environments not being fully designed for integrated digital delivery.
Limited availability of specialist assistive technologies for higher-need learners.
Difficulties balancing practical learning with extended screen-based tasks for some SEND learners.
Staff confidence varying in adapting digital content for accessibility and inclusion.

To improve accessibility further, the faculty would benefit from:

Increased assistive technology availability and awareness.
More consistent accessible resource design across curriculum areas.
Improved workshop-based digital infrastructure.
Additional staff CPD around adaptive digital teaching strategies.
Simplified and more consistent digital learner journeys across platforms and systems.

Teams/DLE: MS Teams is currently used across the faculty as a central platform for communication, assignment distribution, learning resources, online submissions, learner support, and blended delivery. Staff use Teams to share lesson materials, PowerPoints, videos, workshop guidance, revision resources, and assessment information. It is also used to support remote access to learning, tutorial activity, and communication between staff and learners.

The platform is functioning well overall and has become embedded within day-to-day delivery. Learners are generally confident accessing Teams for assignments, messages, and learning materials.

Strengths include:

Consistent communication between staff and learners.
Centralised access to learning resources and assignments.
Support for blended and independent learning.
Improved tracking of learner engagement and submissions.
Integration with Microsoft 365 tools.

Current gaps and challenges include:

Inconsistent use across curriculum areas and individual staff.
Variable learner engagement with independent online learning.
Connectivity limitations in workshop spaces affecting live use.
Some practical areas lacking screens or devices to fully integrate digital teaching.
Need for further development of interactive and industry-linked digital content rather than static resource storage.
Greater integration needed between Teams, digital portfolios, and practical workshop delivery.

Overall, Teams provides a strong foundation, but continued development of digital pedagogy, infrastructure, and consistency would further improve learner outcomes.

Additional comments: One of the main challenges within construction and building services is balancing practical, hands-on delivery with meaningful digital integration. Learners still require significant workshop time to develop technical competence, therefore digital tools must enhance delivery rather than replace practical learning.

There is also a growing gap between rapidly evolving industry technologies and the pace at which education environments can update equipment, software, and staff training. Emerging areas such as renewable technologies, smart systems, AI, BIM, and digital diagnostics are becoming increasingly important within industry, and continued investment will be essential to ensure learners remain industry-ready.

Consistency is another challenge. While there is strong digital practice across many areas, approaches can vary between staff and curriculum teams. Continued CPD, clearer expectations, and improved infrastructure would help embed a more consistent learner experience across the faculty.

Finally, workshop environments often present practical barriers to digital delivery due to connectivity, device availability, and room design. Improving these areas would significantly strengthen blended learning and independent learner development.

Self-reported barrier impact (1=significant, 5=none):
Device access barrier: Moderate impact
Software barrier: Some impact
Staff capability barrier: Moderate impact

strengths:
Clear evidence that digital development is taking place. Platforms such as MS Teams, Learning Assistant, Smart Assessor, online learning resources and digital testing equipment now embedded in day-to-day delivery.

Staff readiness: Strengths: Strong use of digital portfolio systems and online evidence tracking. Increasing use of digital teaching resources. Good engagement with Teams and blended learning.
Gaps: Consistency in embedding advanced digital skills. AI tools confidence. Industry-standard software.

What's working: There is clear evidence that digital development is taking place across the faculty, and learners are regularly exposed to a range of digital systems and technologies through their programmes. Platforms such as MS Teams, Learning Assistant, Smart Assessor, online learning resources, digital testing equipment, and manufacturer guidance are now embedded within day-to-day delivery and assessment practices.

However, there is an important distinction between learners using digital tools and learners genuinely developing digital capability. In many cases, digital systems are currently being used as operational tools to support delivery, administration, evidence collection, communication, or efficiency, rather than as part of a fully planned strategy to intentionally build learners' long-term digital skills and independence.

For example:

Learners upload evidence digitally, but this does not always mean they understand wider digital organisation, data management, or professional digital working practices.
Learners access Teams and online resources, but independent digital research and critical evaluation skills are still developing.
Staff increasingly use digital platforms to streamline teaching and assessment, though the learner digital skill development behind these activities can sometimes be implicit rather than explicit.
Digital technologies are often used to support completion and compliance, rather than fully preparing learners for rapidly changing digital expectations within industry.

That said, there are positive examples emerging:

Learners are becoming more confident using online technical resources, digital communication tools, and manufacturer guidance.
Digital portfolios are improving learner ownership of assessment evidence and progress tracking.
Some curriculum areas are naturally embedding industry-relevant technologies, particularly around diagnostics, testing equipment, and digital documentation.

The key next step is moving from "digital use" to "digital fluency," where digital skill development becomes intentional, visible, progressive, and directly linked to employability, problem solving, independent learning, and modern industry expectations.

Consistency in embedding advanced digital skills across all curriculum areas.
Confidence using AI tools effectively and safely within education and industry contexts.
Wider use of industry-standard software and emerging technologies linked to modern construction methods and smart technologies.
Staff confidence in developing higher-level learner digital independence rather than relying on tutor-led approaches.
Continued CPD needed around cyber awareness, digital safeguarding, and evolving industry technologies.

afiPhases.title: Address audit-identified priorities
afiPhases.description:
Support requested by area: The biggest area of support needed is not simply access to more devices, software, or hardware -- it is support to strategically plan, embed, and deliver digital fluency within both classroom and workshop environments.

Technology on its own does not automatically improve learning. Without clear curriculum planning, staff confidence, and ongoing support, digital tools risk becoming little more than administrative systems or isolated activities rather than meaningful drivers of learner development. To have real impact, digital skills need to be intentionally mapped into schemes of learning, workshop practice, assessment, employer expectations, and progression pathways.

Support is therefore needed in several connected areas:

Structured curriculum planning support to help staff identify where digital fluency naturally fits within practical and theoretical delivery.
CPD focused not just on "how to use the technology," but how to teach and develop digital capability progressively and meaningfully.
Guidance on embedding digital skills within workshop activities, fault finding, diagnostics, technical research, communication, AI literacy, and problem solving.
Time for curriculum teams to collaboratively design digital learning approaches and share best practice.
Leadership and strategic direction to ensure digital development is consistent, visible, and aligned to industry expectations.
Ongoing coaching and support for staff to move from digital compliance to digitally enhanced pedagogy.
Reliable infrastructure, devices, software, and workshop connectivity to allow digital approaches to happen seamlessly and consistently.

The ultimate goal should be creating digitally fluent learners who can confidently operate within modern industry environments, rather than learners who have simply used digital systems during their course. This requires a long-term cultural and curriculum shift, not just investment in technology alone.

activityLog.notes:
HoA Digital Skills & Infrastructure Audit response — submitted by Richard Hanney.

Core digital skills for learners: Digital Communication & Professional Conduct
Learners must be able to communicate professionally using email, MS Teams, online learning platforms, and digital job application systems. This includes submitting work digitally, responding to employer communications, and using collaborative platforms safely and professionally.
AI Literacy & Safe Use of Digital Tools
Learners should understand how to use AI tools responsibly to support learning, problem-solving, revision, planning, and research, whilst recognising issues around accuracy, plagiarism, safeguarding, and professional ethics within the construction and building services industries.
Digital Documentation & Data Handling
Learners need confidence using digital systems such as online portfolios, digital markbooks, QR-based evidence collection, spreadsheets, and cloud storage. They should be able to upload evidence, track progress, complete digital forms, and interpret basic performance or project data.
Industry-Specific Digital Systems & Software
Learners should develop competence in sector-specific technologies such as digital testing equipment, smart diagnostic tools, CAD/drawing interpretation software, online regulations/resources, Smart Assessor, Learning Assistant, and digital installation manuals used within construction and building services.
Digital Problem Solving & Technical Research
Learners must be able to independently research technical information online, interpret manufacturers' guidance, use digital fault-finding resources, and safely access current regulations, standards, and installation guidance relevant to their trade area.

How skills are baseline assessed: Digital skills are currently assessed through a range of methods across the learner journey:

Initial assessment and induction activities to identify learner confidence with digital platforms, email, Teams, and online systems.
Observation of learners using systems such as Learning Assistant, Smart Assessor, online submissions, and digital learning resources during lessons and workshop activity.
Portfolio evidence uploads and completion of digital assignments/projects.
Employer and work placement feedback linked to professionalism, communication, and use of workplace technology.
End-of-unit practical assessments where learners use digital tools, online guidance, testing equipment, or digital documentation.
Ongoing tutorial discussions and progress reviews identifying learner confidence and development needs in digital skills.
Tracking engagement with online learning platforms, independent study resources, and digital enrichment activities.

Staff capability needed: Staff need a strong blend of general and industry-specific digital capability to deliver these skills effectively, including:

Confidence using digital teaching platforms such as MS Teams, Learning Assistant, Smart Assessor, and online assessment systems.
Ability to model professional digital communication and digital working practices expected within industry.
Competence using sector-specific digital tools, testing equipment, diagnostic systems, online regulations, and digital manufacturer resources.
Understanding of AI tools and how to integrate them safely and ethically into teaching, learning, assessment, and learner support.
Ability to troubleshoot common learner digital issues and support learners with varying levels of digital confidence.
Skills in digital assessment, online feedback, evidence tracking, and use of data to monitor learner progress.
Confidence embedding digital skills naturally into workshop and classroom delivery rather than treating them as standalone activities.

Potential impact if barriers resolved: If the current barriers around devices, infrastructure, software access, and staff development were addressed, the impact on learner outcomes could be significant across both achievement and long-term employability.

Most importantly, digital development could move from being largely operational and compliance-driven to becoming intentional, progressive, and fully embedded within curriculum design and industry preparation. Learners would not simply be using digital tools because they have to -- they would be developing genuine digital fluency linked to modern workplace expectations.

This would lead to:

Greater learner independence, confidence, and problem-solving ability.
Stronger preparation for apprenticeships, employment, and progression into increasingly digital industries.
Improved learner engagement through more interactive, visual, and industry-relevant learning experiences.
Better integration of modern construction technologies such as renewable systems, smart diagnostics, BIM, digital reporting, and AI-supported workflows.
More consistent digital experiences across curriculum areas and teaching teams.
Improved accessibility and support for learners with SEND or lower digital starting points.
Increased opportunities for blended learning, revision, remote access, and flexible support.
Stronger employer confidence in learners' readiness for modern working environments.

Support requested: The biggest area of support needed is not simply access to more devices, software, or hardware -- it is support to strategically plan, embed, and deliver digital fluency within both classroom and workshop environments.

Technology on its own does not automatically improve learning. Without clear curriculum planning, staff confidence, and ongoing support, digital tools risk becoming little more than administrative systems or isolated activities rather than meaningful drivers of learner development. To have real impact, digital skills need to be intentionally mapped into schemes of learning, workshop practice, assessment, employer expectations, and progression pathways.

Support is therefore needed in several connected areas:

Structured curriculum planning support to help staff identify where digital fluency naturally fits within practical and theoretical delivery.
CPD focused not just on "how to use the technology," but how to teach and develop digital capability progressively and meaningfully.
Guidance on embedding digital skills within workshop activities, fault finding, diagnostics, technical research, communication, AI literacy, and problem solving.
Time for curriculum teams to collaboratively design digital learning approaches and share best practice.
Leadership and strategic direction to ensure digital development is consistent, visible, and aligned to industry expectations.
Ongoing coaching and support for staff to move from digital compliance to digitally enhanced pedagogy.
Reliable infrastructure, devices, software, and workshop connectivity to allow digital approaches to happen seamlessly and consistently.

The ultimate goal should be creating digitally fluent learners who can confidently operate within modern industry environments, rather than learners who have simply used digital systems during their course. This requires a long-term cultural and curriculum shift, not just investment in technology alone.

Assistive technology: Microsoft Immersive Reader (within Teams / OneNote / Office), Built-in text-to-speech tools (Windows / Microsoft 365), Speech-to-text / dictation tools (Microsoft 365 Dictate), Reader / scribe support - Anything additional, usually funded by the employer.

name: Engineering (General)
context:
Devices available: College PCs, laptops, 3D printers

Devices needed: High-spec teaching laptops / staff workstations, Smart teaching spaces (interactive screens + capture), Network/cloud licensing (MATLAB, CAD, PLC ), Workshop teaching constrained by non-portable or fixed AV equipment

Network/connectivity: Network/Wi-Fi limitations in practical spaces

Software available: Microsoft Teams, Microsoft 365 (Word, Excel, PowerPoint, OneDrive, SharePoint, Outlook, MS Project), Smart Assessor, Programming environments (Python, C/C++), SolidWorks, Minitab, Ansys, Granta Edupack, Festo Fluid Sim,Cura, V-Carve, Fusion 360, GX Works 2, Siemens TIA Portal, ABB Robot Studio, Ingus Robot Control, Creality Print, Ultimaker Cura Enterprise, Multisim, Thinker CAD, GasTurb

Software gaps: Software access restrictions (on-campus only, not apprentice-friendly), Inconsistent software availability across labs and cohorts, Restricted or insufficient licences for high-demand tools

Assistive technology: Microsoft Immersive Reader (within Teams / OneNote / Office), Built-in text-to-speech tools (Windows / Microsoft 365), Speech-to-text / dictation tools (Microsoft 365 Dictate), Reader / scribe support - Anything additional, usually funded by the employer.

Accessibility barriers: Technology is often the primary communication channel -- reliability is critical, Staff training on assistive technology is non-negotiable, not optional, Avoid unexpected platform changes without notice, Dyslexia & SpLD, Multiple means of engagement -- not just text, not just video, Check Accessibility tool in Microsoft Office -- build this into staff CPD

Teams/DLE: Hosting teaching materials and project briefs, Managing class communication and announcements, Supporting live online delivery and hybrid sessions, Facilitating project-based collaboration (e.g. Project Week activity) - GAPS: Inconsistent Team/Channel structures across modules, Teams used more for communication than structured pedagogy - Dependency on staff digital confidence and consistency - Manual enrolment / permissions management issues - Lack of seamless integration with timetabling and registers (EBS) - Limited automation of student onboarding to Teams spaces

Additional comments: Some learners rely on recorded sessions uploaded to Teams/VLE rather than fully supported lecture capture systems, Recordings are created using staff laptops rather than dedicated classroom infrastructure, with known technical limitations

Self-reported barrier impact (1=significant, 5=none):
Device access barrier: Significant impact
Software barrier: Significant impact
Staff capability barrier: Moderate impact
Network barrier: Low impact

name: Health (inc. Health T-Level)
context:
Devices: Laptops, iPads, VR, PCs, responsive manikins.

Network: Wi-Fi reliability inconsistent across some teaching and simulation spaces. Connectivity issues occasionally interrupt simulation technology and online registers.

AT available: Microsoft Immersive Reader, Read&Write, speech-to-text/dictation, captioning, font adjustments, screen reading, translation.

Accessibility barriers: Some learners struggle due to low literacy, processing difficulties, or limited digital confidence. Learners with dyslexia, ADHD, autism find heavily text-based platforms difficult. Inconsistent use of AT by staff.

Devices available: Laptops, ipads, vr, pc's, responsive manikins

Devices needed: Reliable staff and learner laptops = many existing devices are slow, unreliable, or unable to effectively run modern teaching platforms and healthcare simulation software
Simulation technology upgrades = improved simulation manikins, digital observation systems, tablets for simulation activities, and updated healthcare technology equipment
Mobile devices for teaching and assessment = iPads/tablets to support digital observations, electronic documentation, quizzes, and interactive learning activities
Interactive classroom technology = upgraded screens, visualisers, microphones, and hybrid teaching equipment to improve engagement and accessibility
Industry-standard healthcare software access = greater access to electronic patient record training systems such as EMIS or SystmOne simulation environments

Network/connectivity: Wi-Fi reliability is inconsistent across some teaching and simulation spaces, which impacts the use of Teams, online learning platforms, and digital teaching activities.
Connectivity issues occasionally interrupt simulation technology, online registers, and cloud-based systems, reducing lesson flow and learner engagement.
Mobile signal is poor in some teaching areas, limiting backup communication methods and impacting authentication systems or mobile-based learning activities.

Software available: Microsoft 365 suite = Outlook, Teams, Word, Excel, PowerPoint, OneDrive, SharePoint and Forms
Virtual Learning Environment (VLE) = access to online learning materials, assignment submission, feedback, and course resources
Microsoft Teams = online lessons, communication, collaboration, tutorials, assignment support, and virtual meetings
Simulation software and healthcare technologies = simulation manikin software, digital observation systems, and scenario-based simulation tools
Online assessment and tracking systems = e-portfolios, online submissions, progress tracking, and attendance monitoring systems
Healthcare-specific digital systems = exposure to electronic patient record concepts and healthcare documentation platforms where available
Digital research platforms = online journals, NHS resources, NICE guidance, and academic databases for evidence-based learning
Interactive learning platforms = quizzes, polls, and digital engagement tools used within lessons
AI-enabled tools = limited exposure to AI-supported learning and productivity tools for research, revision, and planning activities
Cloud-based collaboration tools = shared documents, collaborative presentations, and digital group working through Teams and OneDrive

Software gaps: Access to training versions of Electronic Patient Record (EPR) systems = EMIS, SystmOne, Epic, and Rio environments to better prepare learners for placement and employment
Expanded simulation software and immersive healthcare technology = advanced simulation platforms, VR clinical scenarios, and digital patient monitoring systems to replicate real healthcare environments
Digital medication management systems = access to electronic prescribing and MAR chart software used within NHS and care settings
Telehealth and remote care platforms = systems used for virtual consultations, remote monitoring, and digital patient communication, reflecting modern healthcare practice
Healthcare data analysis and reporting tools = improved access to Excel-based dashboards, Power BI, and healthcare data reporting systems to support analytical and decision-making skills
AI-supported healthcare learning tools = safe educational AI platforms to support research, clinical reasoning, reflective practice, and digital literacy development
Online anatomy and clinical skills platforms = interactive software to support physiology, anatomy, and clinical procedure teaching through visual and digital learning methods

Assistive technology: Microsoft Immersive Reader = text-to-speech, reading support, translation, and accessibility adjustments within Microsoft platforms
Read&Write support tools = literacy support, reading assistance, dictation, and vocabulary support for learners with additional needs
Speech-to-text and dictation tools = voice typing within Microsoft Word and accessibility features across Windows devices
Accessibility tools within Microsoft 365 = captioning, font adjustments, screen reading, translation, and accessibility checking features

Accessibility barriers: Some learners struggle to access digital resources due to low literacy levels, processing difficulties, or limited confidence using technology independently.
Learners with dyslexia, ADHD, autism, and cognition/learning difficulties can find heavily text-based platforms, poorly organised Teams channels, and inconsistent digital layouts difficult to navigate.
Inconsistent use of accessibility tools by staff means support is not always embedded routinely into teaching practice.

Teams/DLE: Microsoft Teams is currently used primarily for communication, assignment distribution, resource sharing, and online delivery where required.
Usage across the department is inconsistent, with significant variation in how effectively staff utilise Teams Class environments.
Some staff use Teams effectively to organise learning materials, communication, assignments, and collaborative activities, while others use it only at a very basic level.

Self-reported barrier impact (1=significant, 5=none):
Device access barrier: Some impact
Software barrier: Moderate impact
Staff capability barrier: Moderate impact
Network barrier: Low impact
strengths:
Simulation technology and scenario-based learning demonstrating strong impact. Teams and online resource access allowing learners to revisit materials independently. Digital quizzes improving engagement.

Staff readiness: Mixed — small number of digitally confident staff, larger proportion needing support with basic digital fluency. Most comfortable with basic Outlook and Teams but confidence drops with more advanced features. Clear gaps in Excel, SharePoint/OneDrive, Teams Class environments.

What's working: Simulation technology and scenario-based learning = learners demonstrate increased confidence, communication skills, and clinical decision-making during practical assessments and placement activity
Microsoft Teams and online resource access = learners are able to revisit lesson materials, recorded content, and assignment guidance independently, improving accessibility and revision opportunities
Digital quizzes and interactive learning tools = improved learner engagement, participation, and knowledge recall during theory-based lessons
Use of online research platforms and NHS resources = learners are developing stronger evidence-based practice skills and producing higher-quality written work and assignments
Digital submission and feedback systems = learners receive feedback more quickly and are better able to track progress and act on developmental targets

Staff readiness: Staff confidence is currently mixed across the department, with a small number of digitally confident staff and a larger proportion requiring support with basic digital fluency.
Most staff are comfortable with basic day-to-day communication tools such as Outlook and Teams, but confidence drops significantly when using more advanced digital features or troubleshooting learner issues.
There are clear gaps in confidence using Excel, digital data tracking, collaborative platforms such as SharePoint/OneDrive, and embedding digital activities meaningfully into teaching practice.
Confidence with AI tools and AI literacy is currently low. Many staff have limited understanding of how AI can be used safely and effectively within education and healthcare contexts.

auditIntel.staffReadiness:
Staff confidence is currently mixed across the department, with a small number of digitally confident staff and a larger proportion requiring support with basic digital fluency.
Most staff are comfortable with basic day-to-day communication tools such as Outlook and Teams, but confidence drops significantly when using more advanced digital features or troubleshooting learner issues.
There are clear gaps in confidence using Excel, digital data tracking, collaborative platforms such as SharePoint/OneDrive, and embedding digital activities meaningfully into teaching practice.
Confidence with AI tools and AI literacy is currently low. Many staff have limited understanding of how AI can be used safely and effectively within education and healthcare contexts.
auditIntel.capabilityNeeded:

Digital communication skills = professional use of email, Teams messaging, calendars, video calls, and collaborative working tools
Confidence using healthcare-related digital systems = basic exposure to electronic patient record systems, simulation software, and digital observation tools
Digital organisation and file management = saving, sharing, locating, and organising files correctly using OneDrive and SharePoint

name: Sport
context:
Devices: College laptops (quality and reliability inconsistent). LapSafe when available. Force plates and speed gates. Dartfish (not currently used — could save money).

AT available: Microsoft accessibility tools — Immersive Reader, dictation/speech-to-text, captions, screen magnification. Additional SEND support available but not always embedded consistently.

Accessibility barriers: No known significant accessibility barriers. Some learners may need support with organisation, confidence, reading large amounts of text, locating resources on Teams.

Devices available: Learners currently have access to college laptops, although the quality and reliability of these can be inconsistent. There is also access to a LapSafe, which provides a more reliable set of devices when available.

In terms of specialist sport equipment, learners can access testing equipment such as force plates and speed gates. These are useful for collecting performance data, although they mainly output numerical results rather than offering a full digital analysis platform. This means learners can practise recording and interpreting data, but there is still a need to improve access to higher-quality devices and more sport-specific digital tools.

Devices needed: Specialist cameras (VEO)
Heart rate/GPS vests
iPads or tablets for use in sports hall 

Not planned: Large screen or portable display for practical spaces
A screen in the gym would allow staff to model video analysis, show VEO footage, review technique, display session plans and give whole-group feedback.

Network/connectivity: All ok

Software available: Got most of what we need. We have dartfish we dont use, this could save money.

Software gaps: The main software gap is coach planning software. We will have access to VEO analytics for performance analysis, but we do not currently have a simple digital platform for learners to plan, adapt and evaluate coaching sessions.

This would improve session planning, reflection, coaching portfolios and assessment evidence, while helping learners work in a more professional, industry-relevant way.

Assistive technology: Currently available support includes Microsoft accessibility tools such as Immersive Reader, dictation/speech-to-text, captions, screen magnification and accessibility settings within Teams/Office 365.

Learners can also access additional SEND support through college systems where required, but this is not always embedded consistently within Sport delivery.

Accessibility barriers: There are no known significant accessibility barriers currently preventing learners from accessing digital resources in Sport.

However, some learners may still need additional support with organisation, confidence, reading large amounts of text, logging in, locating resources on Teams, or using accessibility tools effectively. The main improvement is making digital resources more consistent, clearly structured and easier to access.

Teams/DLE: Teams is used as the main digital learning environment for Sport, including resources, assignments, communication and learner evidence. However, the current Teams structure is not organised consistently enough, and channels are not always used effectively.

A key area for improvement is to create a clearer, standardised Teams structure across courses, with channels used properly for units, resources, assignments, announcements and support. This would make content easier for learners to find, reduce confusion, and improve accessibility, especially for learners who need more structure and guidance.

Additional comments: The main barriers are device quality, organisation of Teams, access to technology in practical spaces, and staff confidence in modelling digital skills. Sport delivery is often practical, so digital tools need to work reliably in gyms, sports halls and outdoor spaces, not just classrooms.

Improved devices, better connectivity, clearer Teams structures, access to coaching planning software and staff training on VEO, heart-rate monitors and digital assessment would all help improve teaching, learner access and assessment evidence.

Self-reported barrier impact (1=significant, 5=none):
Device access barrier: Moderate impact
Software barrier: Low impact
Staff capability barrier: Some impact
Network barrier: No impact
strengths:
Learners completing online tasks through Teams — allows staff to monitor engagement and completion. Gives clear record of learner activity.

Staff readiness: Staff part way through the journey. Most can use core systems competently. Main gap is confidence in modelling digital skills clearly for learners, and using sport-specific technology in coaching.

activityLog.notes:
HoA Digital Skills & Infrastructure Audit response — submitted by Dan Pratlett.

Core digital skills for learners: Sport learners must develop digital skills in data handling, video performance analysis, use of heart-rate monitoring and fitness testing equipment, professional digital communication, AI literacy and digital content creation. This includes using VEO, heart-rate monitors, fitness testing equipment, Excel, Teams, SharePoint, online research tools, AI tools and digital presentation/content creation platforms. 

These skills are essential for progression from Level 2 to Level 3 Sport, as well as future routes into coaching, personal training, sport science, academy sport, fitness instruction and sport development. Learners need to be able to collect and interpret performance data, review video footage, communicate professionally, use AI ethically, evaluate online information and create digital evidence that reflects current practice in the sport industry.

How skills are baseline assessed: These skills have only been partially baseline assessed at this stage. General digital skills have been checked informally through induction and early course activity, including learners accessing Teams, Outlook, online timetables, shared files and digital assignment platforms.

Learners' research skills, online communication and ability to complete digital work have also been identified through early classroom tasks, assignment preparation and submitted work.

However, the sport-specific technology skills have not yet been baseline assessed, as learners have not yet had access to, or structured use of, specific equipment such as VEO, heart-rate monitors or performance/fitness testing equipment. As a result, there is currently no reliable baseline for learners' starting points with these tools.

Staff capability needed: Sports Staff need to be confident enough to:
-Use Teams, SharePoint, Outlook and digital assignment systems properly, including setting work, giving feedback and supporting learner submissions.
-Use VEO and heart-rate monitors themselves before expecting learners to use them.
-Set up and manage basic fitness testing equipment, then help learners record and interpret the data accurately.
-Support learners with basic digital issues, such as logging in, accessing files, uploading work and using shared documents.
-Teach safe and ethical use of AI, including how to use it for ideas, research and revision without copying or producing inaccurate work.
-Create and model simple digital evidence, such as videos, presentations, screenshots, data tables and reflective logs.

Potential impact if barriers resolved: If these barriers were resolved, learners would have a more consistent and professional digital learning experience. Better devices, clearer Teams structures, improved access to software and targeted staff CPD would make resources easier to access, reduce lost learning time and improve learner confidence.

It would also improve the quality of coaching plans, digital portfolios, performance analysis, practical assessment evidence and learner reflection. Overall, this would support better engagement, stronger assessment outcomes and smoother progression into Level 3, coaching, fitness and sport-related employment.

Support requested: The most useful support would be targeted staff CPD on sport-specific digital tools, improved devices, better connectivity in practical spaces, and support to standardise Teams.

AI use has already been delivered to the team by the Head of Sport, so the next priority is embedding this consistently in lessons and assessment. Staff would benefit most from practical training on VEO, heart-rate monitors, digital assessment, Teams structure and modelling digital skills. Learners would benefit from more reliable laptops/tablets, clearer access to resources, and software that supports coaching planning, reflection and digital portfolios.
